nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments, reef slopes, and lagoons with moderate light and water movement

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis and generally requires no target feeding. Some sources suggest the colony may benefit from supplemental micro-plankton, baby brine shrimp, or invertebrate foods in lower-light systems, though this is not widely considered necessary in well-lit reef aquariums. Supplemental trace elements, strontium, and iodine are commonly recommended by hobbyists.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: SPS corals in close proximity in small tanks
other soft corals in direct contact

Хомелка; 3872x2592. Named Cladiella colti in a public aquarium; whole finger-leather colony with extended polyps clearly shown. Slight particulate/glass haze.
Хомелка; 3872x2592. Named Cladiella colti, alternate angle of the same aquarium colony. A blue tang intrudes at lower-right and there is some water haze.
natomik; ~1024px. Sharp in-situ colony with polyp-covered finger lobes in mixed brown and cream/white tones, adding color variety.

summary: Cladiella, widely sold as Colt Coral or Finger Leather Coral, is a fast-growing Indo-Pacific soft coral broadly regarded as one of the most beginner-friendly reef corals available. It tolerates moderate conditions but produces allelopathic compounds that can stress neighboring soft corals.
careNotes:
Cladiella is a large genus of roughly 67 recognized species, all commonly traded under the names Colt Coral or Finger Leather Coral. The genus is closely related to Klyxum, a genus split established in 2000, and the two share near-identical aquarium husbandry requirements — hobbyists and retailers frequently use the names interchangeably.

Cladiella is generally regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly soft corals in the reef hobby. Its leathery, finger-like lobes extend when conditions are favorable and fully retract when disturbed. The surface tends to be sticky due to significant mucus production, which is a normal defense response.

Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised — roughly 50–150 PAR. These corals tend to adapt well to a range of light levels, making them forgiving for hobbyists without advanced lighting setups. Sudden changes in light intensity are best avoided.
• Flow: Moderate to medium-strong flow is commonly suggested. Polyps should visibly sway. Avoid directing laminar flow straight at the colony base.
• Placement: Cladiella can be placed anywhere in the aquarium, from lower rockwork to midwater areas, making positioning straightforward.
• Chemical allelopathy: Cladiella has been reported to produce allelopathic compounds (chemical substances released into the water) that can harm neighboring soft corals, particularly Sinularia and Sarcophyton species. Running activated carbon is commonly recommended in systems housing multiple soft coral genera. In smaller tank volumes, this chemical pressure may affect SPS corals (hard, stony corals) over time as well.
• Growth: These corals tend to grow rapidly under good conditions and may need occasional pruning. Cuttings (frags) attach readily to rubble or frag plugs and propagate easily.
• Shrinkage: Temporary shrinkage lasting days to weeks is normal and not always a sign of distress. Colonies typically re-expand fully after minor disturbances or water changes.
• Copper sensitivity: As with all corals and invertebrates, copper-based medications should be avoided in any system housing Cladiella.
• Nitrate tolerance: Nitrate levels of 1–2 ppm are commonly suggested for best coloration and polyp extension, though these corals have been reported to tolerate a wider range than most SPS corals.
• Coloration: Typically cream, pale grey, or muted brown/green, with extended polyps sometimes displaying a contrasting green or brown hue.
breedingNotes: Asexual propagation via fragmentation is straightforward and widely practiced. Cuttings can be made with clean scissors or a razor blade and attached to live rock rubble or frag plugs; success rates have been reported as very high. The coral also reproduces naturally in aquariums by dropping branches that settle and form new colonies. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Cauliflower Colt Coral
primary.notes: Listed as Klyxum sp. (formerly Cladiella). Provided temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, flow medium-strong, lighting moderate-high, care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive, placement all areas.

additional.name: Quality Marine – Cladiella Colt Coral
additional.notes: Lists as Cladiella sp. Cross-checked water parameters: temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. Agreed on moderate-high lighting and moderate-strong flow. Care level easy.

additional.name: ReefTrak – Colt Coral (Cladiella pachyclados)
additional.notes: Lists scientific name as Cladiella pachyclados. Confirms temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.3, SG 1.023–1.025. Provides native range: Indo-Pacific from Red Sea and East Africa to Great Barrier Reef and Indo-Malaysian waters. Minimum tank 20 gallons (76L).

additional.name: Reef Aquarium – Colt Coral
additional.notes: Confirms semi-aggressive temperament, easy care level, allelopathy/chemical defense (activated carbon recommended), adult colony size ~12–14 inches, recommended nitrate 1–2 ppm. Lighting listed as moderate to low-moderate.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Cladiella and Klyxum (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: Family Alcyoniidae, Order Malacalcyonacea. Confirmed genus Cladiella (Gray, 1869) and its relationship to Klyxum. Common names Colt Coral and Finger Leather Coral confirmed. Not used for husbandry parameters.
